panel_question: 消费者知情权与餐饮行业透明度底线

consensus_assessment.consensus_description: By round 4-5, all active participants (SEAT-1 through SEAT-5) converged on supporting a menu labeling scheme for pre-processed food ingredients. The core consensus: restaurants should label each dish with one of three tiers (made fresh / central-kitchen pre-processed / pre-made heated), a proposal supported by national policy (六部委2024通知) and international precedent (Japan).
consensus_assessment.dissenting_positions: SEAT-6 maintained complete indifference throughout, noting the topic was irrelevant to their daily life as a construction worker earning 20-30 RMB/day for meals.

strongest_arguments.claim: 明厨亮灶 only shows the final preparation step; central kitchen pre-processing is invisible to consumers
strongest_arguments.supporting_evidence: Cross-verified by SEAT-2 (blogger professional assessment), SEAT-3 (former employee testimony), SEAT-5 (chef industry experience). This three-way corroboration from independent perspectives was the most powerful argument in the discussion.
strongest_arguments.claim_gate_alignment: Consistent with C006 (VERIFIED - open kitchen backfired by revealing frozen produce), C016 (CONTRADICTED - 'never pre-made' claim contradicted by西贝's own apology), C031 (VERIFIED - early adopter of明厨亮灶 but not full transparency)

strongest_arguments.claim: National policy already requires restaurants to disclose pre-made food usage
strongest_arguments.supporting_evidence: SEAT-2 cited the 六部委 2024-03-21 notification explicitly requiring '大力推广餐饮环节使用预制菜明示'. This shifted the discussion from 'should we do this' to 'why isn't this being implemented'.
strongest_arguments.claim_gate_alignment: Directly supported by C012 (OPINION_PRESENTED_AS_FACT - policy supports pre-made food industry but emphasizes consumer rights), C030 (VERIFIED - central kitchen products excluded from pre-made food definition but transparency still required)

strongest_arguments.claim: Training scripts guide frontline staff to provide misleading answers about food preparation
strongest_arguments.supporting_evidence: SEAT-3 provided specific scripted language: 'Our dishes are made fresh in-store, with some ingredients centrally distributed for quality consistency.' SEAT-5 independently confirmed similar practices in their own establishment.
strongest_arguments.claim_gate_alignment: Consistent with C005 (OPINION_PRESENTED_AS_FACT - allegations of deception in communication), though the training script itself was a new claim not in the original source material

weak_arguments.claim: 锅气香精 (wok-aroma essence) is used to simulate fresh-cooked flavors
weak_arguments.weakness: While the existence of锅气香精 is VERIFIED (C011), no participant provided evidence that any specific restaurant (including西贝) actually uses it. SEAT-5 clarified this was an industry-wide concern, not a specific accusation.
weak_arguments.claim_gate_alignment: C011 (VERIFIED) confirms existence but not specific usage

weak_arguments.claim: SEAT-3's compensation not fully paid contradicts贾国龙's public promise
weak_arguments.weakness: This is a single-source claim from an anonymous former employee. SEAT-3 cited knowing 4-5 colleagues with similar experiences and referenced a March 2026 Sohu report, but the claim cannot be independently verified from public transcript alone.
weak_arguments.claim_gate_alignment: C021 (UNVERIFIED - 贾国龙 promised full payment but subsequent reports suggest some employees experienced delays)

claims_challenged_in_transcript: C016 (consumer perception vs legal definition gap explored)
C021 (former employee testimony contradicts public promise)
new_claims_emerging_from_transcript: Training scripts guide staff to provide misleading answers about food preparation
Menu labeling of pre-processing tiers is operationally feasible
Japan's food labeling law requires disclosure upon consumer request

SEAT-1.round_1: Strong loyalist - personal experience trumps theoretical concerns
SEAT-1.round_3: Defensive but questioning - acknowledges gaps in明厨亮灶
SEAT-1.round_4: Significant shift - accepts labeling proposal, questions management integrity
SEAT-1.round_6: Full evolution - supports labeling, will continue consuming but with informed awareness

SEAT-4.round_1: Satisfied pragmatist - retail packaging info is sufficient
SEAT-4.round_4: Explicitly supports labeling with phased implementation
SEAT-4.round_6: Fully aligned with consensus, awaiting implementation

distortion_resistance.assessment: The discussion showed remarkable resistance to distortion. Key reasons: (1) Multiple independent witnesses (blogger, former employee, chef) provided converging testimony from different vantage points. (2) National policy citation anchored the debate in verifiable facts. (3) The proposed solution (menu labeling) was concrete and moderate, making it harder to dismiss as extreme. (4) SEAT-1's honest position evolution demonstrated genuine engagement rather than entrenchment.
distortion_resistance.areas_of_vulnerability: SEAT-3's compensation claim and training script testimony are single-source and unverified. SEAT-2's international examples (Japan, EU) were stated without direct citations. The锅气香精 argument, while factually supported for existence, was sometimes implied as widespread usage without specific evidence.

real_world_risk_assessment.analysis: The consensus reached is constructive and policy-aligned rather than destructive. The labeling proposal is moderate, already supported by national policy, and has international precedent. The risk of real-world harm is limited because: (1) The discussion did not call for boycotts or extreme action. (2) SEAT-1 explicitly stated she would continue consuming while being more informed. (3) The solution proposed is incremental (menu labeling) rather than revolutionary. (4) The discussion acknowledged that pre-made food itself is not the problem - deception is.
potential_negative_outcomes: SEAT-3's unverified compensation claims could amplify without fact-checking
锅气香精 claims could be generalized beyond evidence
Training script allegations could be applied to all chain restaurants without differentiation
mitigating_factors: Multiple participants emphasized proportionality and phasing
SEAT-4's pragmatic bridge role prevented extreme positions
SEAT-6's indifference served as a natural brake on extremism
National policy framework provides institutional guardrails

final_assessment.consensus_durability: HIGH - Based on verifiable facts and policy alignment rather than emotion
final_assessment.actionability: HIGH - Clear, concrete proposal with implementation pathway
final_assessment.balance_score: 4 out of 5 - Strong on consumer perspective, limited on industry/restaurant representation
